title: Modern contraception usage vs. average years of schooling for women
subtitle: Share of married or co-habiting women who use at least one modern method of contraception vs. the average years of formal education that women between 15-64 years have attended.
note: "Modern contraception methods" include oral hormonal pills, the intra-uterine device (IUD), male or female condoms, injectables, the implant, female and male sterilization, vaginal barrier methods, or emergency contraception.
caption:
Modern contraception usage vs. average years of schooling for women

Share of married or co-habiting women who use at least one modern method of contraception vs. the average years of formal education that women between 15-64 years have attended.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the average years of schooling for women aged 15-64 and the share of married or co-habiting women aged 15-49 using modern contraception methods, which include oral hormonal pills, the intra-uterine device (IUD), male or female condoms, injectables, the implant, female and male sterilization, vaginal barrier methods, or emergency contraception. The horizontal axis ranges from approximately 3 to 11 years of schooling, while the vertical axis measures contraception usage from 0% to 70%. Data points are color-coded by continent, with Africa represented in purple, Asia in teal, North America in salmon, South America in dark red, Europe in blue, and Oceania in light blue. Circle sizes correspond to population, with a reference scale indicating sizes for 100 million and 250 million people.

Several African nations cluster in the lower-left quadrant, indicating lower values for both metrics. Niger sits near 3.5 years of schooling and roughly 14% usage. Mozambique, Cote d'Ivoire, and Benin group between 4.5 and 6.5 years of schooling with usage rates hovering around 25%. Kenya and Uganda appear in the mid-range, with schooling between 6 and 7 years and usage rates near 40% to 58%. Lesotho is positioned higher on the vertical axis at approximately 65% usage with about 8 years of schooling.

In Asia, Bangladesh and Indonesia are represented by large teal circles, indicating substantial populations. Bangladesh aligns with roughly 8.5 years of schooling and 55% usage, while Indonesia sits near 10 years of schooling and 50% usage. Tunisia appears slightly below Indonesia in usage but similar in schooling years. Saudi Arabia is positioned near 10.5 years of schooling with usage around 33%. Mexico, a large salmon circle representing North America, is located near the top right, showing approximately 10.5 years of schooling and nearly 70% usage. Cuba appears at the far right with over 11 years of schooling and 60% usage. The visual distribution shows a general trend where higher average years of schooling correspond with higher percentages of modern contraception usage across the labeled countries.
